tldr: Xata is a managed serverless Postgres platform; GORM is an open-source Go ORM, not a database, so this migration is really about moving off Xata's managed service to a self-hosted (or third-party) Postgres accessed through GORM. Plan for two parallel tracks: relocate the data, then introduce GORM as the new data-access layer in your Go services.
why_switch: Eliminate per-database vendor pricing by self-hosting Postgres behind GORM
Standardize a Go-first data access layer with idiomatic models and migrations
Reduce lock-in: GORM works with any Postgres/MySQL/SQLite backend
Gain finer control over schema, indexes, and connection pooling
Open-source stack with permissive licensing and large community
what_to_export: Sequence current values and identity columns
Views, materialized views, and stored functions if used
Roles, grants, and row-level security policies
File attachment metadata and any binary blobs
Database branches you want to preserve as separate environments
export_method: Use a standard Postgres-compatible export. From Xata, obtain a Postgres connection string for the database/branch you want to move and run pg_dump against it: pg_dump --no-owner --no-privileges --format=custom -d "$XATA_PG_URL" -f xata.dump. For very large databases, dump per-schema or per-table to parallelize. If a direct Postgres connection is not available for your workspace, fall back to the Xata CLI or HTTP API to export each table to CSV/JSON, plus a separately captured schema definition. Verify row counts per table after export and store the dump in a secure location. Keep the source database read-only during cutover by pausing writers, or take an online dump and replay a final delta with logical replication if downtime must be minimized.
import_method: Provision a target Postgres instance (managed service or self-hosted) sized for your workload. Restore the schema and data with pg_restore --no-owner --no-privileges -d "$NEW_PG_URL" xata.dump (or psql for plain-SQL dumps). Then introduce GORM in your Go service: go get gorm.io/gorm and the Postgres driver gorm.io/driver/postgres, open a connection with gorm.Open(postgres.Open(dsn), &gorm.Config{}), and define struct models that match the existing tables. Use struct tags to map column names, primary keys, and indexes; avoid AutoMigrate on production data and instead generate migrations from your existing schema. Replace Xata SDK calls with GORM queries (First, Find, Where, Create, Updates, Transaction). Validate query parity by running a representative test suite against both old and new endpoints before flipping traffic.

concept_mapping.note: 1:1 conceptual mapping at the Postgres layer
Branching is not a GORM/Postgres native primitive
GORM does not handle blobs; store files in S3-compatible storage

gotchas: GORM is not a database; you still need a Postgres host. Confirm hosting choice before starting.
Xata-specific column types or extensions may not exist on your target Postgres; review extensions before restore.
AutoMigrate can drift from existing schema. Prefer explicit SQL migrations on production.
File attachments and search are platform features, not pure Postgres. Replan these as separate services.
Connection pooling differs: configure sql.DB SetMaxOpenConns/SetMaxIdleConns explicitly under GORM.
Branch-based workflows in Xata require a new strategy (per-environment databases or schema-per-branch).

email.subject: Heads-up: we are migrating off Xata in the next 14 days
email.body:
Hi,

We are moving our database off Xata to a self-hosted Postgres accessed via GORM in our Go services. Cutover will happen within the next 14 days.

What changes for you:
- The application URL and features stay the same.
- A short read-only window is expected during final data sync; we will announce the exact timestamp 48 hours before.
- Any direct Xata API tokens you hold will stop working after cutover. If you rely on them, please reply so we can issue replacements against the new database.

What does not change:
- Your data, accounts, and integrations.
- Pricing and SLAs on our side.

If you have questions or custom integrations to coordinate, reply to this email and we will work through them with you.

Thank you for your patience as we improve the platform.

Your customer success team
